text.title: Competitive Analysis (UX) — 6-Step Process
text.summary: Structured procedure for examining competitor products across predefined criteria and converting observations into prioritized design recommendations.

section.title: Step 1: Define Scope and Select Competitors

rule.statement: Select 3-5 direct competitors, 2-3 indirect competitors, and 1-2 aspirational examples. Cap total at 8 to maintain analysis depth.
rule.rationale: More than 8 competitors degrades analysis depth — breadth replaces insight. Aspirational examples from adjacent industries expose solutions that direct competitors have not discovered.

section.p: Competitor types: Direct (same problem, same user — e.g. Uber vs Lyft). Indirect (same problem, different approach — e.g. Uber vs public transit). Partial (overlapping features). Aspirational (best-in-class to learn from, not a competitor).
section.reference: Analysis plan skeleton with competitor list and criteria

section.title: Step 2: Define Evaluation Criteria

rule.statement: Establish evaluation criteria before examining any competitor product — criteria derived after examination are biased toward what was observed.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc criteria selection produces confirmation bias — analysts unconsciously weight criteria where their preferred design performs well. Pre-defined criteria are neutral.

section.p: Dimensions to consider: features (what can users do), user flows (how users accomplish goals), UI patterns (design conventions used), information architecture (content organization), content and tone, accessibility compliance.

section.title: Step 3: Gather Data by Actually Using Products

rule.statement: Create real accounts and complete full user flows rather than reviewing screenshots or marketing pages.
rule.rationale: Screenshots reveal visual design only. Friction, error states, loading behavior, and onboarding quality are only visible through direct use. Surface-level review misses the most actionable findings.

section.p: Data collection: use on both desktop and mobile, complete key user flows end-to-end, document edge cases, contact support, test accessibility.

rule.statement: Use the same documentation structure for every competitor — annotated screenshots, flow steps, strengths, weaknesses, and opportunities. Inconsistent documentation prevents comparison.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent notes cannot be compared across competitors. A structured template forces coverage of the same criteria for all products.

rule.statement: After documenting all competitors, identify: what everyone does (baseline expectation), what nobody does (opportunity gap), what top performers do distinctively (best practice), and what common problems exist (anti-patterns to avoid).
rule.rationale: Pattern analysis across competitors is where individual observations become strategic insight. Analyzing one competitor at a time produces a list of features, not a competitive landscape.

rule.statement: Categorize every recommendation into Must Have (table stakes), Should Have (competitive parity), Could Differentiate (opportunity), or Avoid (observed failure mode).
rule.rationale: Flat recommendation lists produce debate. Four-tier categorization produces a prioritization decision: Must Have items are blockers; Avoid items are negative signals; differentiation opportunities are the most valuable output.
